digital_id.note: Estonia's mandatory chip-based e-ID card has been required for all citizens/residents since 2002 (99% coverage) and is used for authentication, legally binding digital signatures (equivalent to handwritten signatures under eIDAS), and access to virtually all e-government and banking services via the X-Road data-exchange layer (about 2.2 billion transactions/year across 52,000+ organizations). Architecture is decentralized/citizen-controlled: identity credentials and two cryptographic key pairs reside on the citizen's own PIN-protected chip rather than a single central database queried at each transaction, and X-Road itself is a distributed exchange layer connecting separate databases rather than one central repository.

sources.title: Estonia's X-Road: The Decentralized Digital Backbone of a Paperless Nation | Digital Government Network

cash_payment.note: The euro has been Estonia's sole legal tender since January 1, 2011, and cash remains fully legal and in circulation. Eesti Pank (the central bank) actively guarantees continued cash availability and exchanges old Estonian kroon banknotes/coins for euros indefinitely; no restrictions on ordinary cash payments were found.

sources.title: Eesti Pank guarantees the existence of cash | Eesti Pank

mandatory_schooling.note: School attendance is compulsory (ages 7–17), but homeschooling is legal and regulated under the Education Act: parental-choice homeschooling is permitted only up to age 12 (parent-managed and parent-financed), while medical-necessity homeschooling can continue through age 17 (state-funded, school-managed); both require annual school approval and at least twice-yearly assessment against the curriculum. A May 2026 amendment (Amendment Paper 583) grants the government broad new regulation-making power over homeschooling conditions, passed without consultation with homeschooling families; detailed regulations are still to be drafted.

sources.title: Estonia Home Education Requirements 2026 | Starpath Learning

social_credit.note: No government social-credit system exists. Private credit bureaus such as Creditinfo Eesti provide financial creditworthiness scores (the probability a person will fail to pay invoices) only, unrelated to any state-run citizen trustworthiness score.

sources.title: Credit Score on Private Person | Creditinfo Eesti

alt_currency.note: Cryptocurrency ownership and trading are legal. From July 1, 2026, crypto-asset service providers must hold a license from Finantsinspektsioon (or another EU regulator) under the EU's MiCA regulation and Estonia's Crypto Asset Market Act — legacy VASP licenses issued under the older AML regime become invalid after that date. No restrictions on gold or precious-metals ownership were found.

sources.title: Estonia Crypto Licensing 2026, Updated AML, VASP Rules | Global Law Experts

mandatory_insurance.note: Health insurance is compulsory and solidarity-funded through the Estonian Health Insurance Fund (EHIF/Tervisekassa), financed mainly by a 13% payroll social tax paid by employers. Coverage reaches about 95% of the population; the remainder is still covered for emergency care regardless of insurance status.

sources.title: Health insurance | Tervisekassa (Estonian Health Insurance Fund)

travel_restrictions.note: Domestic and EU/Schengen freedom of movement is unrestricted for Estonian residents generally, but Estonia has tightened its Russian border: short-term Schengen-visa tourism/leisure entry by Russian citizens has been banned since September 19, 2022 (with exceptions for close family), the disputed 'Saatse Boot' area was closed in 2025 after armed Russian soldiers were sighted there, and the Koidula and Luhamaa border crossings have been closed nightly (19:00–07:00) since February 24, 2026, for security reasons.

sources.title: Restrictions on accepting visa applications for Russian and Belarusian citizens | Välisministeerium (Estonian Ministry of Foreign Affairs)
Procedure for crossing the Estonian-Russian border | Politsei (Estonian Police and Border Guard)

internet_restrictions.note: General internet access is open, and Estonia scores highly on internet-freedom assessments, but under EU sanctions related to Russia's invasion of Ukraine, Estonia blocks access to Russian state-affiliated broadcasters and has gone further than the EU's own list — as of 2024 the Consumer Protection and Technical Regulatory Authority (TTJA) was blocking roughly 307 websites and 53 TV channels (e.g., Ntv.ru, Ren.tv, 1tv.com, Lenta.ru, Tass.ru).

sources.title: Estonia bans access to Russian state media online | Baltic News Network
Estonia: Freedom on the Net 2024 Country Report | Freedom House

free_speech.note: Estonia ranks 3rd of 180 countries in RSF's 2026 World Press Freedom Index, behind only Norway and the Netherlands, retaining a 'good' rating despite dropping from 2nd place. Media are independent, though RSF flags growing political pressure on the public broadcaster ERR, a media market concentrated among a few groups (Postimees, Ekspress, ERR), and rising online harassment of journalists as practical challenges.

sources.title: Estonia ranks third in 2026 World Press Freedom Index | Estonian World
